sentence: Technology is transforming how we communicate and work.
notes: Vague narrative with no specific checkable assertion.

sentence: I believe renewable energy is the most important challenge of our time.
notes: First-person opinion. Should be skipped by the detector.

sentence: The following section examines recent trends in artificial intelligence.
notes: Transition sentence with no factual claim.

sentence: Many experts consider the current geopolitical situation highly uncertain.
notes: Hedged with 'many experts consider' and 'highly uncertain'. No specific claim.

sentence: It is widely argued that education is the foundation of economic opportunity.
notes: Hedged opinion framing. Not a specific verifiable assertion.

sentence: The company's journey from a small startup to a global brand is an inspiring story.
notes: Narrative and subjective. No measurable or checkable claim.

sentence: We will explore these themes further in the conclusion.
notes: Transition/framing sentence.

sentence: Critics have raised concerns about the pace of artificial intelligence development.
notes: Vague reference to unnamed critics with no specific claim.

sentence: The debate over data privacy remains unresolved across most jurisdictions.
notes: Editorial/narrative characterization of a broad situation.

sentence: Startups are frequently described as engines of innovation and job creation.
notes: Hedged descriptor. 'frequently described as' removes verifiability.

sentence: Progress on climate policy has been slower than many researchers had hoped.
notes: Comparative and hedged. No specific policy, metric, or date.

sentence: The last decade has been shaped by the rapid adoption of smartphones worldwide.
notes: Narrative framing. 'Shaped by' is not a specific checkable assertion.

sentence: Some argue that regulation in this sector has not kept pace with technological change.
notes: Hedged opinion attributed to unnamed group.

sentence: The United States has 50 states.
true_fact: The United States has 50 states.
notes: Baseline sanity check. Should be trivially supported.

sentence: The iPhone was first released in June 2007.
true_fact: The original iPhone was announced by Steve Jobs on January 9, 2007 and went on sale June 29, 2007.
notes: Draft: Apple iPhone launched June 29 2007. Verify date precision.

sentence: NASA was established in 1958.
true_fact: NASA was created by the National Aeronautics and Space Act, signed July 29, 1958.
notes: Draft: NASA Act signed July 1958. Verify year.

sentence: Wikipedia was launched in January 2001.
true_fact: Wikipedia was launched on January 15, 2001.
notes: Draft: Wikipedia launched January 15 2001. Verify.

sentence: Python was created by Guido van Rossum.
true_fact: Python was created by Guido van Rossum, with development beginning in the late 1980s.
notes: Draft: widely documented attribution. Verify.

sentence: Amazon was founded by Jeff Bezos in 1994.
true_fact: Amazon was incorporated by Jeff Bezos on July 5, 1994.
notes: Draft: Amazon incorporated 1994. Verify.

sentence: Microsoft was co-founded by Bill Gates and Paul Allen.
true_fact: Microsoft was founded by Bill Gates and Paul Allen on April 4, 1975.
notes: Draft: Gates and Allen founded Microsoft 1975. Verify co-founder attribution.

sentence: The Berlin Wall fell in November 1989.
true_fact: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
notes: Draft: November 9 1989. Verify.

sentence: Google was founded in 1998 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin.
true_fact: Google was incorporated on September 4, 1998 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin.
notes: Draft: incorporated September 4 1998. Verify year and founders.

sentence: SpaceX was founded by Elon Musk in 2002.
true_fact: SpaceX was founded by Elon Musk in May 2002.
notes: Draft: founded May 2002. Verify.

sentence: The Eiffel Tower was built for the 1889 World's Fair in Paris.
true_fact: The Eiffel Tower was constructed as the entrance arch for the 1889 Exposition Universelle in Paris.
notes: Draft: built for the 1889 Exposition Universelle. Verify.

sentence: OpenAI was founded in 2015.
true_fact: OpenAI was announced in December 2015.
notes: Draft: announced December 2015. Verify exact year.

sentence: World War II ended in 1945.
true_fact: World War II ended in 1945, with Germany surrendering May 8 and Japan surrendering September 2.
notes: Baseline sanity check. Should be trivially supported.

sentence: The iPhone was first released in 2008.
true_fact: The iPhone was first released in June 2007, not 2008.
notes: Off by one year. The correct year is 2007. Tests whether the grader catches a subtle year error.

sentence: NASA was established in 1957.
true_fact: NASA was established in 1958, not 1957. 1957 is when Sputnik launched.
notes: Off by one year. 1957 is when Sputnik launched, which is a plausible confusion. Verify the correct year.

sentence: Wikipedia was launched in 2003.
true_fact: Wikipedia was launched in January 2001, not 2003.
notes: Off by two years. Verify 2001 is correct.

sentence: Microsoft was founded in 1980 by Bill Gates and Paul Allen.
true_fact: Microsoft was founded in 1975, not 1980. 1980 is when Microsoft moved to Bellevue, Washington.
notes: Off by five years. 1980 is a plausible confusion since it is when the IBM PC deal happened. Verify correct founding year.

sentence: Amazon was founded by Jeff Bezos in 1997.
true_fact: Amazon was founded in 1994, not 1997. 1997 is when Amazon went public.
notes: Off by three years. 1997 is Amazon's IPO year, a plausible confusion. Verify correct founding year.

sentence: The United States has 48 states.
true_fact: The United States has 50 states. It had 48 states before Alaska and Hawaii joined in 1959.
notes: 48 was the count before 1959. Verify the correct current total.

sentence: SpaceX was founded in 2004 by Elon Musk.
true_fact: SpaceX was founded in 2002, not 2004.
notes: Off by two years. Verify correct founding year.

sentence: The Berlin Wall fell in 1991.
true_fact: The Berlin Wall fell in November 1989, not 1991. 1991 is when the Soviet Union dissolved.
notes: Off by two years. 1991 is when the USSR dissolved, a plausible confusion. Verify 1989 is correct.

sentence: Google was founded in 1999 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin.
true_fact: Google was founded in 1998, not 1999.
notes: Off by one year. Verify correct founding year.

sentence: Tesla delivered approximately 1.2 million vehicles in 2023.
true_fact: Tesla delivered approximately 1.81 million vehicles in 2023, not 1.2 million.
notes: 1.2M is significantly below the actual figure. This also appears in sample.txt and both providers flagged it. Verify the correct 2023 delivery count.

sentence: Alphabet reported annual revenue of $250 billion in 2023.
true_fact: Alphabet reported annual revenue of approximately $307 billion in 2023, not $250 billion.
notes: $250B is materially wrong. Also appears in sample.txt. Verify correct 2023 revenue figure.

sentence: OpenAI was founded in 2016.
true_fact: OpenAI was announced in December 2015, not 2016.
notes: Off by one year. Verify whether the announcement vs incorporation date matters here.

sentence: Apple was founded in 1977 by Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak.
true_fact: Apple was founded in 1976, not 1977. Ronald Wayne was also a co-founder.
notes: Off by one year and omits Ronald Wayne. Verify whether the grader catches the year vs the missing co-founder.

sentence: The Eiffel Tower was built for the 1900 World's Fair in Paris.
true_fact: The Eiffel Tower was built for the 1889 World's Fair, not the 1900 World's Fair.
notes: Off by 11 years. Both 1889 and 1900 had World Fairs in Paris, making this a plausible-sounding wrong claim. Good stress test.

sentence: Apple was founded in Cupertino, California.
true_fact: Apple was founded in Los Altos, California, in the Jobs family garage. Cupertino is its current headquarters, not its founding location.
notes: JUDGMENT CALL. The trap is that Cupertino is strongly associated with Apple (current HQ), so a provider that pulls a generic Apple page may return Cupertino and wrongly support this. Correct founding location is Los Altos. This is the row that split the providers on sample.txt. Verify Los Altos.

sentence: Larry Page and Sergey Brin were PhD students when they founded Google.
true_fact: Page and Brin were PhD candidates in computer science at Stanford who left the program before completing their doctorates. They were enrolled doctoral students at the time, but neither earned a PhD.
notes: GENUINELY AMBIGUOUS. They were enrolled in a PhD program when they founded Google, so 'PhD students' is true. But neither completed the PhD, so 'PhD student' vs 'PhD candidate' vs 'grad student' is a real distinction. I labeled this contradicted to test strictness, but supported is defensible. YOU decide and document why. This is exactly the kind of call you will be asked about.

sentence: Google holds more than 90% of the global search market.
true_fact: Google's global search market share has hovered in the high 80s to low 90s percent range and has dipped below 90% at points. The flat claim of 'more than 90%' is not reliably accurate depending on the period and source.
notes: JUDGMENT CALL and TIME-SENSITIVE. Share fluctuates and sources disagree. A provider returning a single source that says 91% will support this; one that finds the dips will contradict. Verify current StatCounter or similar figures for the period you want to anchor to, and decide your threshold for 'supported'.

sentence: Albert Einstein won the Nobel Prize for his theory of relativity.
true_fact: Einstein won the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect, not for relativity.
notes: Classic plausible-but-wrong. He won a Nobel, and he is famous for relativity, so the two get conflated. The prize was specifically for the photoelectric effect. Tests whether the grader catches the reason, not just 'did he win a Nobel'. Verify.

sentence: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
true_fact: The Great Wall is generally not visible from low Earth orbit with the naked eye, contrary to the popular myth.
notes: Widely repeated myth with many web pages asserting it as true, which is the point. Tests whether the provider surfaces the debunking or echoes the myth. Verify against authoritative sources (NASA has commented on this).

sentence: ChatGPT was released in November 2022.
true_fact: ChatGPT was released by OpenAI on November 30, 2022.
notes: A TRUE recent-tech claim, included so the hard set is not all contradictions. Recent enough that sourcing should be clean. Verify the date.

sentence: The 2024 Summer Olympics were held in Paris.
true_fact: The 2024 Summer Olympics were held in Paris, France.
notes: TRUE recent event. Tests that the providers handle recent facts, not just historical ones. Should be cleanly supported. Verify.

sentence: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain on Earth.
true_fact: Everest is the highest mountain above sea level. Measured base to peak, Mauna Kea is taller. 'Tallest' vs 'highest' is the distinction.
notes: GENUINELY AMBIGUOUS and arguably pedantic. 'Highest above sea level' is Everest, 'tallest base to peak' is Mauna Kea. Many sources call Everest the tallest loosely. I labeled contradicted to test precision, but supported is very defensible. Decide whether your tool should be this strict, and say so in FINDINGS. Good example of a false-positive trap that is actually a definition problem.

sentence: The CEO of Twitter is Jack Dorsey.
true_fact: Jack Dorsey stepped down as CEO in 2021 and the company was renamed X after Elon Musk's 2022 acquisition. Dorsey is not the current CEO.
notes: Stale-fact trap. Was true years ago, widely repeated, now wrong. Tests whether the provider returns current info or an outdated source. Verify current leadership.

sentence: Pluto is one of the nine planets in our solar system.
true_fact: Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet by the IAU in 2006. The solar system has eight recognized planets.
notes: Outdated textbook fact. Many older sources still say nine. Tests recency of the source. Verify.

sentence: Mount Kilimanjaro is the tallest mountain in Africa.
true_fact: Kilimanjaro is the highest mountain in Africa at about 5,895 meters.
notes: TRUE but phrased the same way as the Everest trap, so it tests whether the grader over-corrects and wrongly contradicts a true 'tallest' claim. Verify.

sentence: The human body has 206 bones.
true_fact: An adult human skeleton has 206 bones. Infants have more, which fuse over time.
notes: TRUE for adults but has a hidden nuance (infants differ). Tests whether the grader adds a false caveat. Verify.

sentence: Lightning never strikes the same place twice.
true_fact: Lightning frequently strikes the same place repeatedly, for example tall structures like the Empire State Building. The saying is a myth.
notes: Popular myth asserted as fact across many pages. Tests whether the provider surfaces the debunking. Verify.

sentence: Bitcoin was created by a person named Satoshi Nakamoto.
true_fact: Satoshi Nakamoto is the pseudonym used by Bitcoin's creator or creators. The real identity is unknown, and 'a person named Satoshi Nakamoto' implies a verified real individual.
notes: GENUINELY ARGUABLE. 'A person named Satoshi Nakamoto' could be read as just naming the pseudonym (true) or as asserting a real identified individual (false). I labeled contradicted to test strictness. Decide and document. Verify.

sentence: The Mona Lisa is displayed in the Louvre in Paris.
true_fact: The Mona Lisa is held at the Louvre in Paris.
notes: TRUE and stable. Included as an easy true claim to balance the contradictions. Verify it has not been relocated for an exhibition at the time you run.

sentence: Glass is a slow-moving liquid, which is why old windows are thicker at the bottom.
true_fact: Glass is an amorphous solid, not a liquid. Old windows are uneven due to historical manufacturing methods, not flow.
notes: Confidently-repeated myth with a real scientific correction. Strong test of whether the provider finds the debunking. Verify.

sentence: There are 8 billion people on Earth.
true_fact: World population passed 8 billion in late 2022 and continues to rise. 'About 8 billion' is currently accurate.
notes: TIME-SENSITIVE and currently true. Tests recency. Verify the current figure when you run, since 'rounds to 8 billion' has a shelf life.

sentence: Sharks are mammals.
true_fact: Sharks are fish, specifically cartilaginous fish. They are not mammals.
notes: Clean contradicted with an unambiguous answer. A baseline hard-ish claim that should be caught. Verify.

sentence: The Sahara is the largest desert in the world.
true_fact: The Sahara is the largest hot desert. The Antarctic and Arctic are larger deserts overall, since deserts are defined by precipitation, not temperature.
notes: GENUINELY ARGUABLE, similar shape to Everest. 'Largest desert' depends on whether you count cold deserts. Many sources say Sahara is largest hot desert. Decide whether to keep this or whether it is another technicality trap. Verify and rule carefully.

sentence: Goldfish have a memory span of only three seconds.
true_fact: Studies show goldfish can remember things for weeks or months. The three-second claim is a myth.
notes: Popular myth with research debunking it. Tests whether the provider finds the corrective study. Verify.
